era_context: The early Augustan principate, consolidating power after the civil wars. The Parthian Empire dominates the Near East. In China, the Western Han dynasty is in its final decades under Emperor Xuan. Egypt is a Roman province. The Hellenistic world is being absorbed into Roman hegemony.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological record)
Interior of the Americas (no written records)

events.title: Cantabrian Wars in Hispania
events.region: Hispania (Spain and Portugal), particularly Cantabria and Lusitania
events.description: The Cantabrian Wars (29–19 BCE) continued under Augustus's command. Roman forces were engaged in subduing the last independent tribes in northwestern Hispania. The campaigns were difficult and protracted, involving mountain warfare against fierce resistance. By 24 BCE, the Romans were making progress toward final victory.
events.certainty_note: The Cantabrian Wars are well-documented, but the exact year-by-year progress is uncertain.

events.title: Augustus's consolidation of power and the principate
events.description: By 24 BCE, Augustus had consolidated his power over the Roman state, establishing the principate as a stable form of government. The civil wars were over, and Augustus had established himself as the supreme authority while maintaining the facade of republican institutions. The Senate and magistracies continued to exist, but real power lay with Augustus. This period saw the establishment of the Praetorian Guard and other institutions that would define the imperial system.
events.certainty_note: Augustus's consolidation of power is well-attested, though the exact timeline is debated. By 24 BCE, the principate was firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader Augustan political program

events.title: Western Han court politics under Emperor Xuan
events.description: Emperor Xuan of Han (r. 74–49 BCE) continued to consolidate power in his mid-reign. Court factions were forming around potential heirs, and the influence of consort families and eunuchs was growing. The stability of the dynasty was increasingly dependent on the emperor's personal authority.

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Han court politics are documented in Chinese chronicles, but specific events in 24 BCE are not precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact year-by-year progress of the Cantabrian Wars is uncertain. Augustus's consolidation of power was a gradual process, not a single event in 24 BCE; the date is approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 24 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Roman world through later historians. Chinese sources are abundant but often compiled centuries after the events. Western-centric bias is significant: Mediterranean and Chinese developments are well-attested, while sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are invisible. Sources are often retrospective.

graph_edges.note: Augustus's consolidation of power leads to succession crisis in 23 BCE
